\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-200). This chapter records the dedicated substrate
% on which the abelian-group instance of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
% (chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) rests. The genuine residual
% sorry at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean} L235 is
% the abelian-group law on the relative Picard quotient
% \(\Pic(C \times_k T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\); descending tensor product of
% line bundles to that quotient requires a tensor-product operation on
% the underlying carrier and an inverse for invertibles, neither of
% which is presently exposed at the \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) level.
% Mathlib at the pinned commit ships
% \texttt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj} (a tensor product at
% the level of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\)), but does not lift this
% to a monoidal-category structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\). The
% chapter describes the substrate
% \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\), records the lift
% through the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) carrier used by
% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, and pins the consumer
% instance back into \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The chapter is
% project-side and self-contained: dropping a Mathlib upstream PR is
% acceptable but downstream work does NOT wait for the PR to land.

% NOTE (planner directive iter-200). The chapter has three
% responsibilities. First, it records what the Mathlib API surface
% currently exposes at the relevant abstraction levels, what is
% missing, and at which abstraction the project-side construction must
% land. Second, it spells out the construction of
% \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) as a binary operation
% on the category \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), its functoriality, and
% the monoidal-category axioms it has to satisfy in order for the
% downstream Picard-quotient consumer to work. Third, it spells out
% the canonical lift through the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)
% carrier and pins the consumer abelian-group instance on the relative
% Picard quotient back into chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.

\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}

Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
\[
  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
  \qquad
  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
\]
is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.

% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
% groups)
% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
% SOURCE QUOTE:
% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
% abelian groups defined by the formula
% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
% \end{dfn}
%  [\dots]
% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
% topologies  by
% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
%  \end{dfn}"
\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
homomorphisms.}

Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
products and the structure sheaf, so the image
\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
structure. This is the content of
\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.

The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
\[
  \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
  \;:=\; \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
\]
on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three ingredients on the
Lean carrier:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item a binary tensor-product operation
    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\);
  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
    for \(\otimes\);
  \item an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects,
    i.e.\ the dual \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L},
    \mathcal{O}_X)\) of an invertible sheaf.
\end{enumerate}
At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
available; (2) and (3) are present as scheme-level objects, but the
binary operation in (1) that ties them together at the
\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) level is missing. This chapter records the
project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) +
(3) into a monoidal-category structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).

\section{Mathlib API survey}
\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}

The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.

\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
sectionwise tensor product
\[
  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
\]
for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).

\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
tensor-product operation
\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.

\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) together with associator,
unitor, and braiding data. The project's policy on this gap is
\emph{project-side}: the substrate is built inside
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (a fresh file, sketched
\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\), is the
natural home) and the consumer instance is closed against it. A
parallel Mathlib upstream PR with the same content is welcome but not
on the critical path for the project; the consumer is closed against
the project-side substrate independently of any upstream timing.

\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}

\begin{definition}
[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
  \[
    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
  \]
  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
  canonical compatibility
  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
     \;\cong\;
     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.

  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
  \[
    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
  \]
  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
  identities and composition. The functor preserves the structure
  sheaf as a unit, in the sense that there are canonical natural
  isomorphisms
  \[
    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
    \qquad
    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
  \]
  and a canonical associator natural isomorphism
  \[
    \alpha_{M, N, P} \;\colon\;
    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
      \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
  \]
  and a canonical braiding
  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\), each
  inherited from the corresponding data on
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) via the sheafification functor
  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial, has the ring as a
  monoidal unit, and is associative and symmetric up to canonical
  natural isomorphism. These data glue across the cover because the
  underlying gluing data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect
  the canonical morphisms on overlaps. Equivalently, all four pieces of
  natural-isomorphism data (\(\lambda, \rho, \alpha, \beta\)) are
  inherited verbatim from the corresponding data on
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
  sheafification; the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site
  is symmetric monoidal because it preserves the relevant filtered
  colimits and finite limits used to construct the monoidal structure
  on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}
[Monoidal-category structure on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
  \label{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, with unit object
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), associator \(\alpha\), left unitor \(\lambda\),
  right unitor \(\rho\), and braiding \(\beta\) of
  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, satisfies the
  pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms of a symmetric monoidal
  category. Consequently \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) carries a
  canonical symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
  The pentagon and triangle identities (associator and unitor coherence)
  are inherited from the analogous identities on
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\). On any affine open
  \(\Spec A \subset X\), the assertions reduce to the analogous
  identities for the tensor product of \(A\)-modules, which are
  standard. The gluing data on overlaps is preserved by all four
  natural isomorphisms by construction, so the affine-level coherence
  identities yield global coherence identities on
  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Symmetry of \(\beta\) follows by the
  same affine descent argument.
\end{proof}

\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}

The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section spells out how the
monoidal-category structure of
\cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to that subtype, and how
the restriction interacts with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\).

\begin{lemma}
[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Inverse of an invertible module]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
  bundle. The dual
  \[
    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
  \]
  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
  isomorphism
  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
  The contraction map
  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Restriction to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
  \[
    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
    \;\;=\;\;
    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
  \]
  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
  Direct from
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
  monoidal-category structure of
  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to the subcategory by
  the same affine descent argument.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
  functor
  \[
    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
  \]
  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
     \xrightarrow{\sim}
   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
  canonical isomorphism
  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
\end{lemma>

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\) by the
  same descent argument as in
  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}. The compatibility with the
  unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
  same cover.
\end{proof}

\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}

The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
relative Picard quotient via the substrate.

\begin{theorem}
[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}

  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
  % groups)
  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
  % abelian groups defined by the formula
  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
  % \end{dfn}
  %  [\dots]
  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
  % topologies  by
  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
  %  \end{dfn}"
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
  \[
    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
  \]
  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
  abelian-group structure with addition
  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
  inverse
  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
  supplied by the substrate of
  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricted to the
  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier via
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
  map
  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
\end{theorem>

\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
  By
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible},
  the substrate restricts to an abelian-group structure on the set
  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T / \sim\) of isomorphism
  classes of line bundles on \(C \times_k T\). Concretely:
  associativity and commutativity come from the monoidal-category
  axioms of \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} (pentagon, triangle,
  hexagon coherence on isomorphism classes); the neutral element is
  the class of \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (left/right unitor
  coherence); and the inverse is the class of
  \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C
  \times_k T})\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}).

  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback
  functor
  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) is a strong monoidal
  functor; in particular it carries products to products and preserves
  the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism classes
  \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
  homomorphism. Its image is therefore a subgroup of
  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\).

  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set
  of \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of right cosets of this
  subgroup. The quotient by a subgroup of an abelian group is itself
  an abelian group, and the quotient map is a surjective group
  homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup --- the standard
  fact (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the
  general quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra
  textbook). Putting the pieces together gives the
  \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the
  statement, and identifies it with the structure of
  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
\end{proof>

\section{LOC estimates and prover-lane sequencing}
\label{sec:tensorobj_loc_estimates}

The substrate splits into three logical pieces of roughly comparable
size; each is one prover lane in the iter-201+ schedule.

\paragraph{Piece 1: definition + functoriality
(\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}).}
The Lean target file is the new
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}. The body
consists of:
\begin{itemize}
  \item a definition
    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} taking two
    \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) objects and producing one, via
    \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) on the underlying
    presheaves and the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site;
  \item the functorial action on morphisms (bifunctoriality, identity
    and composition laws);
  \item the canonical natural isomorphisms (associator, left/right
    unitor, braiding) inherited from
    \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) under sheafification.
\end{itemize}
Estimate: approximately 80--120 LOC project-side, split as
$\approx 30$--$50$ LOC for the object definition and gluing, $\approx
20$--$30$ LOC for bifunctoriality, $\approx 30$--$40$ LOC for the
natural-isomorphism data.

\paragraph{Piece 2: monoidal-category instance
(\cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}).} The same file, building on Piece
1. The body consists of:
\begin{itemize}
  \item discharging the pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms; each
    reduces affine-locally to the analogous identity on
    \(A\)-modules over a commutative ring \(A\);
  \item assembling the data into a
    \texttt{CategoryTheory.MonoidalCategory} instance with target
    \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and into the symmetric variant
    (the hexagon for the braiding).
\end{itemize}
Estimate: approximately 60--100 LOC project-side, dominated by the
coherence-axiom discharge.

\paragraph{Piece 3: lift + consumer
(\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
       lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
       lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
       lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
       thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}).}
The lift lemmas live in the same
\texttt{TensorObjSubstrate.lean} file; the consumer instance
\(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup\_via\_tensorObj}\)
lives back in
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean}. The body
consists of:
\begin{itemize}
  \item locally-trivial-of-rank-one is preserved by \(\otimes\) and by
    dual on a common trivialising cover (Piece 3a, $\approx 15$--$25$
    LOC);
  \item the dual is a two-sided tensor inverse (Piece 3b, $\approx
    15$--$25$ LOC);
  \item the bifunctor restricts to the
    \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (Piece 3c, $\approx 10$
    LOC);
  \item \(\pi_T^*\) is a strong monoidal functor (Piece 3d, $\approx
    10$--$20$ LOC);
  \item the consumer
    \texttt{addCommGroup\_via\_tensorObj} instance closing the
    file-local sorry of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
    encoding, via \texttt{QuotientAddGroup} on the descended subgroup
    of the abelian group built from Piece 1 + Piece 2 + Piece 3a--3d
    (Piece 3e, $\approx 10$--$20$ LOC).
\end{itemize}
Estimate: approximately 60--100 LOC project-side total.

\paragraph{Total + sequencing.} Roughly 200--320 LOC project-side, conservatively
budgeted as 200--400 LOC for the iter-201+ STRATEGY commitment. The
recommended prover-lane sequence is Piece 1 → Piece 2 → Piece 3,
because:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Piece 2 consumes the data of Piece 1 as typeclass
    hypotheses; closing it before Piece 1 is impossible.
  \item Piece 3a--3d consume the symmetric monoidal-category instance
    of Piece 2 to certify that \(\otimes\) preserves locally-trivial
    rank one (Piece 3a) and that the dual is a two-sided inverse
    (Piece 3b); both depend on the monoidal coherence data.
  \item Piece 3e is the only piece in
    \texttt{RelPicFunctor.lean}; all other pieces live in the new
    file \texttt{TensorObjSubstrate.lean}. Once Pieces 1--3d are in
    place, Piece 3e is a short bridging instance closing the residual
    sorry in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
\end{itemize}

Once Piece 3e lands, the file
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean} collapses from one
honest residual sorry (the body of \texttt{addCommGroup} at L235) to
zero --- the five other declarations
\texttt{PicSharp},
\texttt{PicSharp.functorial},
\texttt{PicSharp.presheaf},
\texttt{PicSharp.etSheaf},
\texttt{PicSharp.etSheaf\_group\_structure}
each consume \texttt{addCommGroup} as a typeclass hypothesis and close
axiom-clean against it via the standard Mathlib patterns documented in
chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, section ``Lean encoding''.
The line at which the file becomes sorry-free completes A.1.c.SubT and
clears the dependency of A.2.c (chapter
\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) on chapter
\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} in a mathematically honest form.

\section{Out of scope}
\label{sec:tensorobj_out_of_scope}

The following are downstream of this chapter and live in dedicated
sibling chapters; this chapter does \emph{not} touch them:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{The sheafified relative Picard functor and its abelian-group target}
    (\cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification,
              thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}) --- once
    \texttt{addCommGroup\_via\_tensorObj} closes the residual sorry at
    \texttt{RelPicFunctor.lean} L235, the
    \(\AddCommGroup\)-valued presheaf, its \'etale sheafification, and
    the sheafification preserving the abelian-group target close
    axiom-clean against it (chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
    section ``Gate annotation (iter-198 refresh)''); none of those
    declarations is the responsibility of this chapter.
  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) by a scheme}
    (A.2.c, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) --- consumes the
    full \(\AddCommGroup\)-valued sheafified presheaf;
    representability is downstream of \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}.
  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3,
    \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}) --- once representability is
    in hand, the identity component is cut out by Hilbert polynomials;
    that is independent of the tensor-product substrate of this chapter.
  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property} (A.4,
    \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) --- consumes the full Route~A stack
    downstream of A.3.
  \item \textbf{Mathlib upstream PR of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)} ---
    the substrate of this chapter is project-side, intentionally; a
    parallel Mathlib upstream PR is welcome but not on the critical
    path. Once the substrate ships in Mathlib, the
    \texttt{TensorObjSubstrate.lean} file can be slimmed to a single
    import + namespace re-export, but no downstream Lean file changes.
\end{itemize}

\section{Internal-consistency check}
\label{sec:tensorobj_consistency_check}

The Lean-pinned declaration blocks of this chapter form a connected
\texttt{\textbackslash uses\{\dots\}} chain whose leaves all resolve
either inside this chapter, inside chapter
\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b on disk), or inside
chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c on disk):
\begin{itemize}
  \item \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} introduces the substrate
    operation and depends on no prior block.
  \item \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} uses
    \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
  \item \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} uses
    \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
          lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}.
  \item \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} uses
    \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
  \item \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} uses
    \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
          lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}.
  \item \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct} uses
    \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
          lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}.
  \item \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj} uses
    \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct} (the restriction to the
    \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) carrier) and implicitly
    \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} from
    \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}.
  \item \cref{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj} uses
    \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
          lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
          lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
          thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
          lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}; the last two are in
    \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b) and
    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c) respectively.
\end{itemize>
All \(\uses\) targets resolve either in this chapter, in
\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, or in
\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. No declaration cross-references a
chapter that does not yet exist on disk.
